title: Декомпозиция монолита: выделение платежного домена
description: Вы — ведущий системный аналитик в компании «EcomSwift» (маркетплейс с 2 млн заказов/мес). Техдиректор поручил выделить платежный модуль из монолита в отдельный микросервис. Срок — 4 месяца. В монолите 500+ таблиц, платежная логика переплетена с заказами, инвентарем и нотификациями.

nodes.context:
Tech lead Elena and CTO Dmitry in kickoff meeting:
«We need to extract payment logic into a separate microservice. Current monolith is 7 years old, payment code is spaghetti. We have 4 months. What's your first step?»

choices.text: «I'll map the bounded context of payments using Event Storming with domain experts, then identify transactional boundaries and anti-corruption layers».
choices.feedback: Correct. Domain-driven design (Event Storming, bounded contexts) is the industry standard for decomposing monoliths. You avoid cutting along technical lines and preserve business semantics.
choices.timeline_event: Analyst initiated Event Storming workshop with domain experts.

choices.text: «I'll copy all payment-related tables and stored procedures into a new database and wrap it with REST API. Two weeks and we're done».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Copy-paste architecture ignores distributed transaction complexity, dual-write problems, and latency. This leads to data inconsistency and hidden coupling.

choices.text: «Let's hire 5 more backend engineers and rewrite everything from scratch in Go. We'll deploy in 2 months».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Big-bang rewrite is the most dangerous migration pattern. 70% of such projects fail or exceed budget by 300%. You ignore incremental migration and business continuity.

nodes.context: You prepared C4 Container diagram for target state. Find the architectural mistake.

context_artifact.data:
C4Context
    title System Context diagram for Payment Service
    
    Person(customer, «Customer», «Buys products»)
    System(monolith, «EcomSwift Monolith», «Legacy»)
    System(payment_svc, «Payment Service», «New microservice»)
    System(bank, «Acquiring Bank», «External PSP»)
    
    Rel(customer, monolith, «Uses», «HTTPS»)
    Rel(monolith, payment_svc, «Calls synchronously», «gRPC»)
    Rel(payment_svc, bank, «Processes payment», «HTTPS»)
    Rel(payment_svc, monolith, «Reads order data directly from monolith DB», «JDBC»)
    
    UpdateLayoutConfig($c4ShapeInRow="3", $c4BoundaryInRow="1")

choices.text: Payment service reads monolith DB directly — creates hidden coupling and bypasses monolith's API layer, making migration impossible to roll back.
choices.feedback: Correct. Direct DB access creates tight coupling. The anti-pattern is called «Shared Database Integration». New service should call monolith's API or use change data capture (CDC).

choices.text: Monolith calls payment service synchronously — should use async events to avoid temporal coupling.
choices.feedback: Not the main error. Sync calls are acceptable during strangler pattern migration phase. Later you can evolve to async. Direct DB access is much worse.

choices.text: No API gateway between customer and monolith — violates security best practices.
choices.feedback: Out of scope. API gateway is a separate concern and not required for payment domain extraction.

nodes.context: Current monolith has tables: `orders`, `payments`, `refunds`, `payment_methods`, `order_history`, `invoice`, `payment_logs`, `failed_jobs`. Payment logic spans 5 tables. Which data should the new payment service OWN (source of truth) vs READ-ONLY from monolith?

choices.text: Payment service owns: payments, refunds, payment_methods, payment_logs. Reads: orders (read-only via API/CDC).
choices.feedback: Correct. Payment aggregate root owns its transaction data. Orders remain in monolith but are read via API or CDC (Change Data Capture). Never dual-write.

choices.text: Payment service owns everything including orders — we'll copy orders table completely.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Copying orders creates data duplication and synchronization nightmare. Payment service should only own payment-related aggregates.

choices.text: Keep all data in monolith, payment service calls monolith's DB for everything — simplest migration.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This defeats the purpose of decoupling. You just added a network hop without domain separation.

choices.text: Payment service owns payments and refunds. Payment_methods stay in monolith (shared with billing). Use synchronous API calls for cross-domain lookups.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Splitting payment_methods across services creates distributed transactions. Payment method is part of payment aggregate, should move together.

nodes.context:
Product owner Maria joins meeting:
«Since you're touching payments, can we also add support for cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, USDT) and loyalty points conversion? Also, our German expansion requires SEPA direct debit — add that too. It's just extra payment methods, right?»

choices.text: «Cryptocurrencies and loyalty points are separate domains with different risk profiles and compliance (FATF, AML). SEPA requires new PSP integration. Let's add them to Q4 roadmap. Current extraction focuses on existing payment methods only».
choices.feedback: Correct. You acknowledged value but protected the critical path. Crypto requires new risk modeling, SEPA needs separate legal agreement. Good scope management.
choices.timeline_event: Analyst prevented scope explosion — crypto and SEPA moved to Q4.

choices.text: «Sure, let's add everything. We'll refactor later if needed».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This guarantees 6+ months of delay. Each new payment method requires legal, compliance, and integration work. You cannot «add later» without rewriting.
choices.timeline_event: Analyst agreed to crypto and SEPA — scope explosion.

choices.text: «No. Focus on extraction. Stop adding features».
choices.feedback: Correct intent but poor delivery. You damaged relationship with product owner. Better to explain trade-offs and defer with timeline.

nodes.context: Order creation currently wraps payment in database transaction. After extraction, order and payment are separate services. Order creation must guarantee: order saved + payment initiated. If payment fails, order should not be created. Order the steps for implementing this using Saga pattern.

choices.text: Order Service creates order with status 'PENDING'
Order Service sends 'CreatePayment' command to Payment Service
Payment Service processes payment, returns payment_id
Order Service updates order status to 'CONFIRMED'
If payment fails, Order Service sends 'CancelOrder' compensating command

nodes.context: You discovered that payment logic inside monolith uses 3 stored procedures that also update `inventory_reservations` table (owned by Inventory domain). Team already started coding new payment service without analyzing this.

choices.text: Stop the line. Call architecture review. Map the cross-domain dependency. Propose event-driven choreography instead of direct DB updates. Add 2 weeks to timeline for anti-corruption layer.
choices.feedback: Correct. Finding hidden coupling before it's too late is senior-level skill. You escalate properly and propose concrete solution with timeline impact.
choices.timeline_event: Analyst discovered hidden coupling with Inventory domain, added 2 weeks for anti-corruption layer.

choices.text: Ignore it. Developers will figure out. We can fix after launch.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This is catastrophic risk. After launch, you'll have dual-writes or data inconsistency. Fixing post-launch requires downtime.

choices.text: Copy the stored procedures into payment service and keep updating both until full migration.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Dual maintenance guarantees divergence and bugs. This is the «distributed monolith» anti-pattern.

nodes.context: You added 2 weeks for anti-corruption layer (step 6). Tech lead is unhappy: «We'll miss the deadline. Can we cut something else?»

choices.text: «We can keep read models in monolith for first release (customer payment history still served by monolith), migrate them in phase 2. Payment writes go to new service only».
choices.feedback: Correct. Incremental migration — writes go to new service first, reads can lag. This is strangler fig pattern. Trade-off is temporary inconsistency, acceptable during migration window.
choices.timeline_event: Team agreed to phased migration: writes first, reads later.

choices.text: «Cut documentation and testing. We'll deliver on time».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Cutting documentation and testing in payment domain is unacceptable for compliance (PCI DSS). This will cause audit failures.

choices.text: «Fire the QA team, we'll test in production».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This is reckless and unprofessional. Payment errors cause direct revenue loss and customer churn.

choices.text: Multiple problems: (1) PCI violation — sending raw card data, should use tokenized payment method; (2) No idempotency key; (3) 200 response lacks payment_id; (4) No async webhook for status updates.
choices.feedback: Correct. Senior analyst must catch PCI compliance (never transmit raw PAN), idempotency for retries, and async pattern for payment confirmation.
choices.timeline_event: Analyst fixed PCI-violating API design before implementation.

choices.text: Only problem is missing idempotency key.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. You missed PCI violation (card number in request) — this is a blocker for production in any regulated environment.

choices.text: Response should be 202 Accepted, not 200.
choices.feedback: Partially correct but not the main issue. 202 is better for async, but PCI violation is critical and immediate.

nodes.context: Audit requires you to prove that every business requirement maps to implementation. Which artifacts must you maintain for traceability?

choices.text: RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix) linking each functional requirement → API endpoint → test case
choices.feedback: Correct. RTM is audit-proof evidence of coverage.

choices.text: Event Storming model (business events → commands → aggregates)
choices.feedback: Correct. Event Storming bridges business language to technical design.

choices.text: ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) for every design choice
choices.feedback: Correct. ADRs explain why decisions were made, critical for post-mortems and onboarding.

choices.text: JIRA tickets only — code is documentation
choices.feedback: Incorrect. JIRA tickets alone don't prove traceability. You need explicit mapping artifacts for audit.

nodes.context: Current monolith processes 500 payments/second at peak (Black Friday). New payment service must handle same load with p99 latency < 100ms. Choose the correct NFR specification.

choices.text: Throughput: 500 tps sustained, 1000 tps burst for 5 min. Latency: p99 < 100ms, p999 < 500ms. Availability: 99.99% (52 min downtime/year).
choices.feedback: Correct. Quantified, testable NFRs with percentiles and availability targets. Burst capacity prevents Black Friday meltdown.

choices.text: Make it fast and reliable.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Vague NFRs («fast», «reliable») are untestable and lead to subjective arguments during acceptance.

choices.text: p99 < 50ms for all requests including database calls.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Unrealistic target (50ms including DB) ignores network latency and external PSP calls. Sets team up for failure.

nodes.context: You need to migrate historical payment data (5 years, 50 million rows) from monolith to new payment service without downtime. Choose the safest approach.

choices.text: Use Change Data Capture (Debezium) + Kafka to stream incremental changes while doing batch backfill with cursor pagination. Dual-write during cutover window.
choices.feedback: Correct. CDC + batch backfill is industry standard for zero-downtime migration. Debezium captures changes during backfill, ensuring consistency.

choices.text: Stop monolith for 8 hours, export all data, import to new service, restart.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. 8 hours downtime for e-commerce during business hours is unacceptable. Revenue loss would be millions.

choices.text: Keep old data in monolith forever, new service starts empty. Old payments show in monolith, new in new service.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Split state confuses customers and reporting. Audit cannot trace full history. Operational nightmare.

nodes.context: You implemented CDC + batch backfill (step 11). Migration completed with 0 downtime and 99.97% data consistency. CTO is impressed and asks: «What's our next architectural target?»

choices.text: «Let's extract Inventory domain using same pattern. I'll run Event Storming next week with warehouse team».
choices.feedback: Excellent. You leverage proven pattern and propose systematic extraction of next domain. Senior-level strategic thinking.
choices.timeline_event: Payment extraction success → Inventory extraction initiated with CDC pattern.

choices.text: «Nothing, we're done. Let's celebrate».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Monolith still has 8 other domains. Stopping after one extraction misses the strategic goal.

choices.text: «Rewrite entire monolith in Rust. We have budget now».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Big-bang rewrite is still dangerous despite one success. Incremental extraction remains safer.

nodes.context: Project complete (4 months, on time). What key lesson should the team document?

choices.text: «Incremental strangler pattern + Event Storming + CDC migration works. Always map bounded contexts before coding, and never share databases».
choices.feedback: Excellent. You captured transferable patterns for future extractions.
choices.timeline_event: Team documented migration playbook for future domain extractions.

choices.text: «Microservices are always better than monoliths. Migrate everything immediately».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This overgeneralization ignores transaction costs, latency, and operational complexity.

choices.text: «We need more junior developers to speed up extraction».
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Senior-heavy work like architecture decomposition is not junior-friendly. Adding juniors would slow down, not speed up.
